binaries.name: jentic
binaries.tagline: discover, inspect, and run against the Jentic API catalog
binaries.short: jentic: discover, inspect, and run against the Jentic API catalog
binaries.long:
jentic is the command-line companion for working with the Jentic API
catalog. Register and switch agent identities, browse and import APIs from
the public catalog into your local registry, inspect operations, and execute
against them.

New here? Run `jentic register` to create an agent, then browse the catalog
with `jentic apis`. To install and operate jentic-one locally, use the
`jenticctl` CLI (e.g. `jenticctl install`). Use `jentic <command> --help` for details.

commands.name: bootstrap
commands.short: Register an agent, wait for approval, and prime your operator — in one step
commands.long:
bootstrap takes a fresh machine from nothing to ready: it registers this
profile as an agent (Dynamic Client Registration), prints an approval link
and waits for a human to approve it, mints and saves tokens, sets the
profile as the default, and generates the Jentic CLI-usage skill into your
agent runtime's native layout.

It is a thin orchestration of `jentic register` and `jentic skill`: nothing
here you can't do by hand, just sequenced so you can start playing right
away. Re-running refreshes everything idempotently.
commands.example:
jentic bootstrap
  jentic bootstrap --operator claude --yes
  jentic bootstrap --profile demo --base-url http://localhost:9000 --all --yes
  jentic bootstrap --skip-skill   # identity only
  jentic bootstrap --dry-run

flags.name: dry-run
flags.usage: show what would happen without registering or writing

flags.name: force
flags.usage: re-register the agent even if the profile already has one (does not overwrite an edited skill block)

flags.name: name
flags.usage: agent client name shown to the approver

flags.name: no-activate
flags.usage: do not set this profile as the default

flags.name: scope
flags.usage: skill placement scope: user or project (default: per-operator)

flags.name: skip-skill
flags.usage: provision identity only; do not write skill files

flags.name: timeout
flags.usage: how long to wait for approval

flags.name: yes
flags.usage: non-interactive: use flags + defaults, no prompts

commands.name: register
commands.short: Register this profile as an agent and obtain tokens
commands.long:
register generates an Ed25519 keypair (if absent), performs Dynamic
Client Registration, waits for an operator to approve the agent, then mints
and saves an access/refresh token pair to the profile. `jentic execute` uses
those tokens as the Authorization bearer.

flags.name: force
flags.usage: re-register even if the profile already has an agent

flags.name: timeout
flags.usage: how long to wait for approval

flags.name: yes
flags.usage: skip the interactive prompt; use flags + defaults

commands.name: profile
commands.short: View and switch the active profile
commands.long:
profile lists the local agent profiles under ~/.jentic/profiles and
switches which one commands act on by default. The active profile is the
--profile flag, else $JENTIC_PROFILE, else config.yaml default_profile.
Run bare on a terminal to pick interactively.

subcommands.name: list
subcommands.short: List profiles and mark the active one

subcommands.name: use
subcommands.short: Set the default profile (interactive picker when no name given)

subcommands.name: add-key
subcommands.short: Create a profile authenticated by an agent API key
subcommands.long:
add-key stores an agent API key (jak_*) generated in the Jentic console as
a new profile. Unlike `jentic register`, no key is generated and no operator
approval is needed — the API key is sent directly as the bearer credential.
Pass --api-key for scripting, or run interactively to be prompted for it.

flags.name: agent-id
flags.usage: agent id this key belongs to (optional, for display)

flags.name: api-key
flags.usage: agent API key (jak_*); prompted when omitted on a terminal

commands.name: logout
commands.short: Revoke and clear the profile's cached tokens

commands.name: catalog
commands.short: Browse, search, and import APIs from the public catalog
commands.long:
catalog explores the Jentic public API catalog and imports specs into
this deployment's local registry. Run bare on a terminal to open an
interactive browser (search, preview operations, import in place); the
subcommands (list/search/show/import/refresh) are script-friendly.
Requires a registered agent (run `jentic register` first).

subcommands.name: list
subcommands.short: List catalog entries

flags.name: all
flags.usage: follow pagination and list every matching entry

flags.name: json
flags.usage: emit JSON instead of formatted output

subcommands.name: search
subcommands.short: Search the catalog by keyword

flags.name: all
flags.usage: follow pagination and list every matching entry

flags.name: json
flags.usage: emit JSON instead of formatted output

subcommands.name: show
subcommands.short: Show a catalog entry and preview its operations

flags.name: json
flags.usage: emit JSON instead of formatted output

flags.name: tag
flags.usage: only preview operations with this tag

subcommands.name: import
subcommands.short: Import a catalog entry into the local registry (auto-promotes to live)

flags.name: json
flags.usage: emit JSON instead of formatted output

flags.name: no-promote
flags.usage: leave imported revisions as draft (do not promote to live)

flags.name: no-wait
flags.usage: enqueue the import and return the job id without waiting

flags.name: timeout
flags.usage: how long to wait for the import job

subcommands.name: refresh
subcommands.short: Refresh the catalog manifest from upstream (requires org:admin)

commands.name: apis
commands.short: Browse and manage APIs in the local registry
commands.long:
apis inspects and manages the APIs imported into this deployment's local
registry — the other half of `jentic catalog import`. Run bare on a
terminal to open an interactive browser (list, preview operations, manage
revisions); the subcommands (list/show/revisions/operations/inspect/
promote/archive/rm/spec) are script-friendly.
Requires a registered agent (run `jentic register` first).

subcommands.name: list
subcommands.short: List locally registered APIs

flags.name: all
flags.usage: follow pagination and list every matching API

flags.name: json
flags.usage: emit JSON instead of formatted output

subcommands.name: show
subcommands.short: Show an API and preview its operations

flags.name: json
flags.usage: emit JSON instead of formatted output

subcommands.name: revisions
subcommands.short: List the revisions of an API

flags.name: all
flags.usage: follow pagination and list every revision

flags.name: json
flags.usage: emit JSON instead of formatted output

flags.name: state
flags.usage: filter by state (draft, published, archived); repeatable

subcommands.name: operations
subcommands.short: List the operations of an API (current revision by default)

flags.name: all
flags.usage: follow pagination and list every operation

flags.name: json
flags.usage: emit JSON instead of formatted output

flags.name: revision
flags.usage: list operations for a specific revision id

subcommands.name: inspect
subcommands.short: Inspect an operation's structural detail

flags.name: format
flags.usage: output format: markdown, json, or openapi

flags.name: revision
flags.usage: pin to a specific revision id

subcommands.name: promote
subcommands.short: Promote a draft revision to live

subcommands.name: archive
subcommands.short: Archive a draft revision

subcommands.name: rm
subcommands.short: Delete an API (or an archived revision)

subcommands.name: spec
subcommands.short: Download the OpenAPI spec for an API

flags.name: out
flags.usage: write to this file instead of stdout

flags.name: revision
flags.usage: download a specific revision id (default: current/live)

commands.name: endpoints
commands.short: Browse the API endpoint + scope reference
commands.long:
endpoints prints every control-plane API endpoint grouped by its typical
caller and the scope(s) it requires. The grouping is an advisory hint — the
scope is the real gate. It reads the server's public endpoint reference
(/reference/endpoints.json, no token needed) — the same join published in
docs/reference/endpoints.md.
Filter with --scope or --actor, or use --json for a machine-readable dump.

flags.name: actor
flags.usage: only endpoints callable by this actor type (user, agent, service_account, toolkit)

flags.name: json
flags.usage: emit JSON instead of formatted output

commands.name: search
commands.short: Search for operations by natural-language query
commands.long:
search finds API operations whose descriptions, names, or paths match a
query. Results are ranked by lexical (full-text) relevance. The
query can also be passed via -q for piping.

Output defaults to JSON when stdout is not a TTY (agent-friendly);
use --json to force JSON on a terminal.
commands.example:
jentic search "list users"
  jentic search -q "create payment" --api stripe.com/api/v1 --limit 5
  jentic search "list pets" --all --json | jq '.data[].operation_id'

flags.name: all
flags.usage: follow pagination and return all results

flags.name: cursor
flags.usage: pagination cursor from a previous response

flags.name: json
flags.usage: force JSON output (default when stdout is not a TTY)

commands.name: inspect
commands.short: Inspect an operation's contract (schema, parameters, examples)
commands.long:
inspect resolves an operation to its full structural detail: HTTP
method, URL, parameters, request/response schemas, and examples. The
target may be a discovered operation's METHOD:url (the same form
`jentic search` prints, e.g. GET:https://rest.coincap.io/v3/markets) or
an opaque operation_id. The output format is negotiated with the
server: JSON for machine consumption, Markdown for human reading.

Default format: JSON when stdout is not a TTY, Markdown when it is.
commands.example:
jentic inspect GET:https://rest.coincap.io/v3/markets --format json | jq .method
  jentic inspect createUser --format markdown
  jentic search "list users" --json | jq -r '.data[0].operation_id' | xargs jentic inspect

flags.name: format
flags.usage: output format: json, markdown, openapi (default: json for non-TTY, markdown for TTY)

flags.name: revision
flags.usage: pin to a specific revision ID

commands.name: execute
commands.short: Execute an operation through the Jentic broker
commands.long:
execute sends an HTTP request through the Jentic broker. The broker
authenticates the caller with their agent token and injects the stored
upstream credential, so the agent token is never sent to the upstream
API directly. The target can be specified in three ways:

  1. METHOD:url — a discovered operation's full URL, the same form
     `jentic search`/`jentic inspect` accept (e.g.
     GET:https://rest.coincap.io/v3/markets). Resolved via inspect, then
     routed through the broker.
  2. operation_id — resolve via inspect, then route through the broker.
  3. METHOD:/path — a broker-relative path sent to --broker-host
     verbatim (e.g. GET:/v1/pets); the caller supplies the broker path.

Path parameters, query parameters, headers, and a request body can be
supplied via flags.

When the broker denies the call (e.g. you are not bound to a toolkit
for the API, or no credential is provisioned), it returns an
agent_directive describing how to recover. execute surfaces that
directive on stderr and exits 2 so a script can branch on the denial.

Exit codes:
  0 — broker returned a non-denial HTTP response (incl. 2xx and upstream errors)
  1 — local/transport failure (DNS, TLS, timeout, connection refused)
  2 — denied by the broker (carries an agent_directive) or resolve failure
      (inspect error, e.g. unknown operation_id)
commands.example:
jentic execute GET:https://rest.coincap.io/v3/markets --json
  jentic execute listPets --query limit=10 --json
  jentic execute GET:/v1/pets/{petId} --path petId=123 --raw
  echo '{"name":"Bob"}' | jentic execute POST:/v1/users --json

flags.name: data-file
flags.usage: read request body from this file

flags.name: raw
flags.usage: stream response body directly to stdout

flags.name: revision
flags.usage: pin to a specific revision ID for inspect

commands.name: access
commands.short: Inspect your access and request more (toolkits, scopes)
commands.long:
access is how an agent closes the gap between having an identity and having
the access to use it. An approved agent starts bound to no toolkits, so its
first execute fails with a 403 telling it to request access. Use this group
to see what you can do now (whoami), ask a human to grant more (request),
and track those requests (list, status, withdraw).

Approval is a human action: filing a request prints an approve_url for your
operator. Output defaults to JSON when stdout is not a TTY (agent-friendly).

subcommands.name: whoami
subcommands.short: Show your agent identity, scopes, and toolkit bindings
subcommands.long:
whoami answers "what can I do right now?" — your agent id, status, granted
scopes, and the toolkits you are bound to. An empty bindings list means you
cannot execute against any API yet; use `jentic access request` to ask.

flags.name: json
flags.usage: force JSON output (default when stdout is not a TTY)

subcommands.name: request
subcommands.short: File a request for a toolkit binding or a scope grant
subcommands.long:
request files an access request for the access you are missing and prints an
approve_url for your human operator. Name the toolkit by the API you found in
search (--toolkit vendor/name), by id (--toolkit-id tk_…), or ask for a scope
(--scope). Use --wait to block until a human decides (or --timeout elapses).

When nothing serves the API yet (a fresh import with no toolkit/credential),
use --provision vendor/name to file the whole path to first execution as one
plan: create a toolkit, provision a credential, bind it (with your proposed
--rules-json), and bind yourself. A human fulfils the create/provision steps
in the dashboard (they enter the secret — it never rides in your request) and
approves. Use --auth to declare the credential type you detected from the spec
(bearer, api_key, basic, oauth2, or none for a no-auth API).

An existing pending request for the same resource is reused, not duplicated.

Exit codes:
  0 — request filed (or, with --wait, fully approved)
  2 — request was denied, expired, or withdrawn (only with --wait)
  3 — still pending when --timeout elapsed (only with --wait)
  4 — partially approved; not all items granted (only with --wait)
subcommands.example:
jentic access request --toolkit httpbin.org/httpbin --reason "smoke test"
  jentic access request --toolkit-id tk_123 --wait
  jentic access request --scope owner:toolkits:read --json
  jentic access request --provision posthog.com/posthog-api --auth bearer \
    --rules-json '[{"effect":"allow","methods":["GET"],"path":".*"}]' --wait

flags.name: auth
flags.usage: credential auth type for --provision: bearer, api_key, basic, oauth2, or none (default bearer)

flags.name: json
flags.usage: force JSON output (default when stdout is not a TTY)

flags.name: provision
flags.usage: file a full provisioning plan to make this API executable (vendor/name[/version])

flags.name: reason
flags.usage: human-readable justification shown to the approver

flags.name: rules-json
flags.usage: proposed permission rules for --provision, as a JSON array

flags.name: toolkit
flags.usage: request a binding to the toolkit serving this API (vendor/name[/version])

flags.name: toolkit-id
flags.usage: request a binding to this toolkit id (tk_…)

flags.name: wait
flags.usage: block until the request is decided

subcommands.name: list
subcommands.short: List your access requests

flags.name: all
flags.usage: follow pagination and return all results

flags.name: cursor
flags.usage: pagination cursor from a previous response

flags.name: json
flags.usage: force JSON output (default when stdout is not a TTY)

flags.name: limit
flags.usage: max results per page (0 = server default)

flags.name: status
flags.usage: filter by status (pending, approved, denied, withdrawn, …)

subcommands.name: status
subcommands.short: Show one access request, including per-item state and approve_url

flags.name: json
flags.usage: force JSON output (default when stdout is not a TTY)

subcommands.name: withdraw
subcommands.short: Withdraw a pending access request

flags.name: json
flags.usage: force JSON output (default when stdout is not a TTY)

subcommands.name: refresh
subcommands.short: Re-mint your token so newly granted scopes take effect
subcommands.long:
refresh forces a fresh token mint, picking up any scopes granted since your
current token was issued. Tokens bake in their scopes at mint time, so after
an approved `scope:grant` request your existing token still can't exercise the
new scope until you refresh. Run this when `jentic access whoami` shows a scope
under "granted" that isn't yet active on your token.

flags.name: json
flags.usage: force JSON output (default when stdout is not a TTY)

commands.name: skill
commands.short: Generate the Jentic CLI-usage skill into your agent's native layout
commands.long:
skill writes a "how to use Jentic via the CLI" skill into each supported
agent runtime's native layout — a dedicated SKILL.md for claude-code,
cursor, and hermes, or a spliced block in AGENTS.md for codex and generic —
so the agent knows the platform loop (register -> request access ->
search/inspect/execute) without you hand-writing anything.

Writes are idempotent: generated content lives in a clearly-marked managed
block, so re-running never clobbers your own edits around it.

subcommands.name: init
subcommands.short: Generate the Jentic skill for one or more operators
subcommands.long:
init detects which agent runtimes you have, lets you pick the targets
(or pass --operator), and writes the Jentic CLI-usage skill into each
one's native layout.
subcommands.example:
jentic skill init
  jentic skill init --operator claude,cursor
  jentic skill init --all --yes
  jentic skill init --operator generic --dry-run

flags.name: force
flags.usage: overwrite a managed block you have manually edited

flags.name: scope
flags.usage: placement scope: user or project (default: per-operator)

subcommands.name: list
subcommands.short: Show supported operators and which are detected in this environment

subcommands.name: remove
subcommands.short: Remove the managed Jentic skill from one or more operators
subcommands.example:
jentic skill remove --operator cursor
  jentic skill remove --operator cursor --dry-run
  jentic skill remove --all --force

flags.name: dry-run
flags.usage: print what would be removed without deleting anything

flags.name: force
flags.usage: remove even a managed block you have manually edited

flags.name: operator
flags.usage: operators to clean up (repeatable or comma-separated)

flags.name: scope
flags.usage: placement scope: user or project (default: per-operator)

commands.name: admin
commands.short: Administer the jentic-one platform
commands.long:
admin groups operator-facing platform administration. Subcommands
require an identity with admin privileges (e.g. org:admin).

`admin config providers` manages runtime credential provider
configuration (e.g. Pipedream) without hand-editing backend YAML or
restarting the server.

subcommands.name: config
subcommands.short: Manage runtime platform configuration

subcommands.name: providers
subcommands.short: Manage credential provider configuration
subcommands.long:
providers configures credential providers (e.g. pipedream) at runtime.
A successful `set` rebuilds the server's provider registry so the change
takes effect without a restart. Secret fields are encrypted at rest and
redacted on read.

subcommands.name: set
subcommands.short: Create or update a provider config
subcommands.long:
set validates the provider-specific fields, encrypts the secret, and
activates the config at runtime. For `pipedream` the recognised flags are
--project-id, --client-id, --environment, and --connect-base-url; the
client_secret is prompted for without echo (or read from stdin with
--secret-stdin).

flags.name: secret-stdin
flags.usage: read client_secret from stdin instead of prompting

subcommands.name: get
subcommands.short: Show a provider config (secrets redacted)

subcommands.name: list
subcommands.short: List provider configs (secrets redacted)

binaries.name: jenticctl
binaries.short: jenticctl: install and operate jentic-one locally
binaries.long:
jenticctl is the installer and lifecycle companion for jentic-one. It
stands up a local deployment (from source via `uv`, or in Docker via
`docker compose`) and manages the running app: health checks, start/stop,
log tailing, updates, and teardown.

New here? Run `jenticctl install` to set up locally. Once installed, use the
`jentic` CLI to register an agent and run against the API catalog. Use
`jenticctl <command> --help` for details on any command.

commands.name: install
commands.short: Interactive wizard to configure and onboard jentic-one
commands.long:
install walks you through choosing a deployment path (from source or
Docker, SQLite or Postgres) and configuration, generates a jentic-one.yaml,
then builds the stack (local venv or Docker image), applies migrations, and
starts the app. Use --skip-build to only generate the config, or --no-start
to build without launching.

flags.name: no-start
flags.usage: don't start the app in the background after a local install

flags.name: no-wizard
flags.usage: don't offer the guided first-run wizard after the stack starts

flags.name: out
flags.usage: path to write the generated config to

flags.name: skip-build
flags.usage: only generate the config; don't build, migrate, or start

commands.name: wizard
commands.short: Guided first-run: create your admin account, connect an operator, and start using Jentic
commands.long:
wizard is the hand-held onboarding that runs after `jenticctl install`. It
waits while you create your first admin account in the browser (so you get
to know the product through the UI), then offers to connect your AI operator
(Claude, Cursor, …): it registers an agent, points you at the console to
approve it, waits for the approval, and finally suggests example prompts you
can try.

Everything it does you can also do by hand (open the UI, `jenticctl setup`,
`jentic bootstrap`); the wizard just sequences it into one continuous flow.
Re-run it any time — each step is idempotent.
commands.example:
jenticctl wizard
  jenticctl wizard --operator claude --yes
  jenticctl wizard --no-open   # don't auto-launch the browser

flags.name: base-url
flags.usage: Jentic control-plane base URL (default: config / http://127.0.0.1:8000)

flags.name: no-open
flags.usage: do not auto-open the browser to the setup page

flags.name: no-wait
flags.usage: do not wait for the admin account; assume it already exists

flags.name: operator
flags.usage: operator to connect (claude, cursor, …); default: auto-detect

flags.name: skip-operator
flags.usage: stop after the account exists; do not connect an operator

flags.name: timeout
flags.usage: how long to wait for account creation and agent approval at each step

flags.name: yes
flags.usage: skip confirmation prompts; connect the detected operator automatically

commands.name: setup
commands.short: Create the first admin account (one-time first-run setup)
commands.long:
setup creates the first administrator for a freshly-installed jentic-one.
It drives the app's one-time create-admin path against the running stack
(Docker compose) or the local install (venv), so the credential never
leaves the operator boundary — there is no default password to rotate.

This succeeds only while no users exist; once an admin is created, manage
further users from the admin UI. Run `jenticctl install` first if you have
not set up locally yet.

flags.name: password
flags.usage: admin password (prompted if omitted; prefer the prompt to keep it out of shell history)

commands.name: reset-password
commands.short: Set a temporary password for a user who is locked out or forgot theirs
commands.long:
reset-password sets a one-time temporary password for an existing user and
forces them to choose a new one at their next sign-in. The operator never
learns the user's standing password — you hand over the temporary one, the
user changes it themselves, and the temporary credential dies on first use.

This is the operator recovery path: there is no email-based self-service
reset. It also clears any login lockout. Drives the app's reset path against
the running stack (Docker compose) or the local install (venv).

flags.name: password
flags.usage: temporary password (prompted if omitted; prefer the prompt to keep it out of shell history)

commands.name: doctor
commands.short: Diagnose the local jentic environment
commands.long:
doctor runs an exhaustive set of health checks across the local jentic
setup — filesystem and config, the recorded install, required tooling, the
control-plane server, and the agent profile — and prints a pass/warn/fail
report with remediation hints.

It is read-only and never mints tokens. It exits non-zero when any check
fails (warnings keep a zero exit), so it is safe to wire into CI.

flags.name: json
flags.usage: emit the check results as JSON

commands.name: status
commands.short: Show install, server, and agent health
commands.long:
status is a health dashboard for the local jentic setup. It reports the
recorded install (mode/db/source), whether the control-plane server is
reachable and its version, and the agent identity/token state for a
profile. It degrades gracefully: missing pieces are reported, not fatal.

commands.name: start
commands.short: Start the configured jentic-one app
commands.long:
start launches the locally-installed jentic-one app in the background.
It requires a completed `jenticctl install` (a generated config and a built
venv); if either is missing it tells you to run `jenticctl install` first.

commands.name: stop
commands.short: Stop the background app or Docker stack
commands.long:
stop terminates the background jentic-one process recorded in
~/.jentic/app.pid (started by `jenticctl install` or `jenticctl start`), or tears
down the Docker stack for a containerized install. It sends SIGTERM, waits
for a graceful exit, then escalates to SIGKILL if needed.

For a Docker install, --volumes also removes the stack's data volumes
(SQLite data or the managed Postgres data dir). Use it to recover from an
incompatible Postgres image upgrade. This destroys the database.

flags.name: timeout
flags.usage: how long to wait for graceful shutdown before SIGKILL

flags.name: volumes
flags.usage: also remove the Docker stack's data volumes (destroys the database)

commands.name: logs
commands.short: View the jentic-one app logs
commands.long:
logs shows the captured output of the locally-installed app
(~/.jentic/logs/app.log). Use --follow to stream new lines as they are
written, or --json to read the structured JSON-lines sink configured by
`jenticctl install`.

flags.name: follow
flags.usage: stream new log lines as they are written

flags.name: json
flags.usage: read the structured JSON-lines sink instead of console output

flags.name: lines
flags.usage: number of trailing lines to show

flags.name: path
flags.usage: print the resolved log file path and exit

commands.name: update
commands.short: Update the jentic CLIs (and check the stack) to the latest build
commands.long:
update reports the installed CLI and server versions, compares the tracked
git ref against its latest commit on GitHub, and (unless --check) rebuilds
and replaces the jenticctl and jentic binaries in place, then rebuilds the
installed stack. Use --cli-only or --stack-only to update just one half.

flags.name: base-url
flags.usage: Jentic control-plane base URL (for the server probe)

flags.name: check
flags.usage: only report status; don't apply any update

flags.name: ref
flags.usage: git ref to update to (default: the installed ref)

flags.name: stack-only
flags.usage: update only the stack (not the CLI binary)

commands.name: uninstall
commands.short: Remove everything under ~/.jentic (config files are backed up to *-old)
commands.long:
uninstall deletes the contents of ~/.jentic (venv, source, data, logs,
profiles, CA, etc.). Config files are preserved: each is renamed to a
'-old' backup so you can restore your settings later. The ~/.jentic
directory itself is kept.

For a Docker install the database lives in a named volume (SQLite data or
the managed Postgres data dir), not under ~/.jentic. uninstall tears the
stack down but PRESERVES that volume by default, so a later `install`
reattaches your data. Use --purge to also delete the data volume
(this destroys the database), or --keep-data to force preservation. With
no flag and an interactive terminal, uninstall asks before deleting it.

On a local (source) install it stops the background app/broker processes
first so they aren't orphaned when their files are removed.

flags.name: keep-data
flags.usage: preserve the Docker stack's data volume (the default; reinstall reattaches it)

flags.name: purge
flags.usage: also remove the Docker stack's data volume (destroys the database)
